note_data.title: Further kinetics
keyPoints: **Rate equation** has the form rate = k[A]^m[B]^n where k is the rate constant and m, n are orders
**Order of reaction** can only be determined from experimental data, not from the stoichiometric equation
**Overall order** is the sum of all individual orders (m + n + ...)
**Rate constant k** has units that depend on the overall order: mol^(1-n) dm^(3(n-1)) s^-1
**Half-life** is constant for first-order reactions and equals ln2/k = 0.693/k
**Rate-determining step** is the slowest step in a multi-step mechanism
**Pre-equilibrium** occurs when fast reversible steps reach equilibrium before the rate-determining step
**Arrhenius equation** ln k = ln A - Ea/RT shows temperature dependence of rate constant
A **catalyst** provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy but does not appear in the rate equation

definitions.definition: An equation that relates the rate of reaction to the concentrations of reactants raised to appropriate powers
The power to which the concentration of a reactant is raised in the rate equation
The proportionality constant in the rate equation that is independent of concentration but depends on temperature
The time taken for the concentration of a reactant to fall to half its initial value
The slowest step in a multi-step reaction mechanism that controls the overall rate of reaction

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the rate equation or order directly without explanation
Give the observation plus the reason using kinetics principles
Use given data to work out order, then write rate equation
Propose a mechanism consistent with the given rate equation

modelAnswer.question: The reaction between compounds P and Q follows the rate equation: rate = k[P]²[Q]. Explain what happens to the rate when the concentration of P is doubled and the concentration of Q is halved.
modelAnswer.answer: When [P] is doubled, the rate increases by a factor of 2² = 4 because P is second order. When [Q] is halved, the rate decreases by a factor of 2 because Q is first order. Overall effect = 4 × 0.5 = 2, so the rate doubles.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Rate increases by factor of 4 when [P] doubled
Mark 2: Rate decreases by factor of 2 when [Q] halved

commonMistakes: Never deduce order from stoichiometric coefficients — order must come from experimental rate data
Students often forget that rate constant units change with overall order
Never say concentration affects rate constant — k is independent of concentration
Students confuse half-life formula: t₁/₂ = 0.693/k only applies to first-order reactions
Never include intermediates in the rate equation — only reactants that appear before or in the rate-determining step
examinerTips: Rate equations appear in every Paper 4 and frequently in Paper 2
Always check units of rate constant match the overall order
For mechanisms, species in rate equation must appear in or before rate-determining step
Use initial rates method or half-life data to determine order experimentally
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Concentration-time graphs showing different orders: zero order (straight line), first order (exponential decay), second order (steeper curve). Also energy profile diagrams showing how catalysts lower activation energy without affecting overall enthalpy change.
